query: pharmacokinetic clearance rates of warfarin enoxaparin fondaparinux and apixaban

retrieved_docs.title: Enoxaparin -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Peak effect of enoxaparin is observed approximately 4 hours after administration. Anti-Factor Xa activity is detected in plasma for about 12 hours. [11] Distribution: The apparent volume of distribution of enoxaparin estimated by anti-Factor-Xa activity is approximately 4.3 Liters. Metabolism: It is metabolized in the liver by desulfation and depolymerization to lower molecular weight fragments with reduced biologic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Molecular-Weight Heparin (LMWH) -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Maximum anti-factor Xa and antithrombin activities occur 3 to 5 hours after subcutaneous administration of LMWH. In healthy subjects, the mean absolute bioavailability of enoxaparin after a 1.5 mg/kg subcutaneous dose is approximately 100%, while dalteparin has an absolute bioavailability of roughly 87%.

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Molecular-Weight Heparin (LMWH) -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Elimination: The total body clearance of enoxaparin is 26 mL/min following intravenous dosing. About 40% of radioactivity and 8% to 20% of anti-factor Xa activity from intravenous dosing are recovered in the urine within 24 hours. The elimination half-life based on anti-factor Xa activity for enoxaparin is approximately 4.5 hours after a single subcutaneous dose and extends to about 7 hours after repeated dosing. The half-life of dalteparin typically ranges between 2 to 2.5 hours after intravenous administration. After subcutaneous administration, longer terminal half-lives (3 to 5 hours) may be observed due to delayed absorption. In patients with chronic kidney disease requiring hemodialysis, the terminal half-life of anti-Xa activity for dalteparin may be prolonged.

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Molecular-Weight Heparin (LMWH) -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: The volume of distribution for enoxaparin's anti-factor Xa activity is approximately 4.3 L. For dalteparin, the volume of distribution for anti-Xa activity ranges between 40 mL/kg and 60 mL/kg. Metabolism: LWMH is metabolized in the liver through desulfation and depolymerization to lower molecular weight fragments with reduced biological potency.

retrieved_docs.title: Dalteparin -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Dalteparin is primarily absorbed subcutaneously. In healthy volunteers, the absolute bioavailability measured as anti-Factor Xa activity was 87±6%. The plasma half-life ranges from 2.4 to 4 hours after subcutaneous and 2 hours after intravenous (IV) dosing. Distribution: The approximate volume of distribution for anti-Factor Xa activity of dalteparin was about 40 to 60 mL/kg. Metabolization: Dalteparin undergoes hepatic metabolism, primarily through desulfation or depolymerization.

retrieved_docs.title: Enoxaparin -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Excretion: Enoxaparin follows first-order kinetics and is eliminated primarily in the urine. The elimination half-life of enoxaparin is approximately 3 to 4.5 hours following a single dose. Following repeated doses, the half-life of enoxaparin increases to approximately 7 hours. As enoxaparin is primarily eliminated by renal excretion, there is a concern for drug accumulation and bleeding risk in patients with renal impairment. [10] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Antithrombotics Around Gastrointestinal Procedures -- Clinical Significance -- Parenteral anticoagulants
retrieved_docs.content: Unfractionated heparin (UFH) administered as a continuous intravenous infusion can be stopped 3 to 4 hours before the procedure, given the short half-life. Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH), including enoxaparin and dalteparin, should be stopped 24 hours before the procedure. [2] [6] Fondaparinux is a synthetic X-a inhibitor that requires discontinuation at least 36 hours preceding a high-risk procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Apixaban -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Elimination: Apixaban undergoes elimination via both urine and feces and has an apparent half-life of 12 hours following oral administration. Among the DOACs, apixaban exhibits 27% renal elimination, and dabigatran demonstrates predominant renal excretion at 80%. Rivaroxaban and edoxaban also undergo renal elimination, but to a lesser extent, at approximately 35% and 50%, respectively. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Rivaroxaban -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Drug-drug interactions: Potentially significant interactions may exist which require dose or frequency adjustment, additional monitoring, and/or consideration of alternative therapy. Consult a drug-interaction database for more detailed information. DOACs like rivaroxaban interact with potent CYP3A inhibitors and P-glycoprotein (P-GP) inhibitors, and breast cancer resistance protein Bcrp (ABCG2) inhibitors (mainly comprising azole-antimycotics, apart from fluconazole and HIV protease inhibitors), which are multi-pathway inhibitors of rivaroxaban clearance and elimination. [15] Its use should also be avoided when using other anticoagulants like unfractionated heparin, low molecular weight heparins (e.g., enoxaparin), fondaparinux, warfarin, apixaban, or dabigatran, except when switching therapy to or from rivaroxaban. Rivaroxaban should be combined with simultaneous use with ASA plus clopidogrel (or other thienopyridines, such as ticlopidine or prasugrel) or plus another adenosine diphosphate (ADP) P2Y12 receptor antagonists (such as ticagrelor) should be avoided. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Apixaban -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Apixaban exhibits dose-linear pharmacokinetics up to a dose of 10 mg. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Andexanet Alfa -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: Andexanet alfa sequesters factor Xa inhibitors such as rivaroxaban and apixaban, and also inhibits the activity of tissue factor pathway inhibitor. The volume of distribution at steady state (Vss) is approximately 4.4 L for the low dose and 3.0 L for the high dose. Metabolism: Andexanet alfa is degraded by proteolytic pathways into small peptides and amino acids.

retrieved_docs.title: Apixaban -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: Apixaban exhibits a plasma protein binding of approximately 87%, indicating high protein binding. In addition, the drug has a volume of distribution of approximately 21 L, suggesting moderate distribution into tissues. Metabolism: Apixaban is primarily metabolized via the enzyme CYP3A4. Approximately 25% of the orally administered dose of apixaban is eliminated in urine and feces as metabolites.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hemostasis -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Iatrogenic Coagulopathy. Medicine is currently in the era of widespread use of antiplatelet agents like aspirin, clopidogrel, ticagrelor and anticoagulants like warfarin, heparin, low molecular weight heparin, rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran, fondaparinux amongst others for various commonly encountered clinical conditions like cardiac stenting/ percutaneous coronary intervention, atrial fibrillation, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, and many more. The way these medications affect the functionality of the various components of clotting cascade can help patients with their clinical conditions. However, it can lead to bleeding/thrombosis in cases of inappropriate dosage, non-compliance, medication interactions, and result in significant morbidity and mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Prothrombin Time -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Argatroban Dabigatran Rivaroxaban Apixaban Edoxaban

retrieved_docs.title: Andexanet Alfa -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: The low-dose protocol consists of a 400 mg bolus administered at a rate of 30 mg/min, followed by an infusion of 4 mg/min for up to 2 hours. The high-dose protocol involves an 800 mg bolus administered at a rate of 30 mg/min, followed by an infusion of 8 mg/min lasting up to 2 hours. If the last rivaroxaban dose was 10 mg or less and was taken less than 8 hours ago, or the time is unknown, administer the low dose. If the last dose of rivaroxaban was more than 10 mg or the amount is unknown, and the time since the previous administration is less than 8 hours or unknown, administer the high dose. If the last dose of apixaban was 5 mg or less and drug administration was less than 8 hours ago or unknown, give the low dose. If the last dose of apixaban was more than 5 mg or unknown, and the drug administration occurred less than 8 hours ago or is unknown, give the high dose. If the last dose of either drug was taken 8 hours or more ago, administer the low dose. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Apixaban -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: The absolute bioavailability of apixaban is approximately 50% for doses up to 10 mg. Consumption of the drug with food does not alter its bioavailability. The peak plasma concentration of apixaban is typically achieved within 3 to 4 hours post-administration.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 573
retrieved_docs.content: The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs is a class that includes unfractionated heparin, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin and tinzaparin, as well as the plasma protein antithrombin III. All of these agents share a common anticoagulant mechanism in which they potentiate antithrombin III to inhibit key clotting factors, thereby preventing thrombus formation. Because of their similar pharmacologic actions, heparin, enoxaparin, dalteparin, tinzaparin and antithrombin III are collectively classified within the Heparin group antithrombotic drugs, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate anticoagulant therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 26
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors and Heparin group antithrombotic drugs are both categories of antithrombotic agents. Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors function by inhibiting factor Xa and comprise agents such as rivaroxaban. The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs includes low‑molecular‑weight heparins—enoxaparin, dalteparin, and tinzaparin—which achieve anticoagulation primarily through potentiation of antithrombin III. While both classes are employed to prevent and treat thrombotic conditions, they differ in their specific mechanisms of action and the individual drugs that belong to each group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1141
retrieved_docs.content: The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs constitute a class of anticoagulant agents that exert their therapeutic effect by potentiating antithrombin III activity, thereby inhibiting clotting factors in the coagulation cascade. This drug class includes several low‑molecular‑weight heparins, specifically dalteparin, nadroparin, and tinzaparin, each of which shares the common mechanism of enhancing antithrombin III–mediated inhibition of thrombin and factor Xa. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same pharmacologic group helps clinicians select appropriate anticoagulant therapy and understand their shared pharmacodynamics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 310
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors constitute a class of anticoagulant agents that exert their therapeutic effect by inhibiting the activity of factor Xa, a key enzyme in the coagulation cascade. Within this pharmacologic group, rivaroxaban is a specific medication that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors class, exemplifying the direct inhibition of factor Xa to prevent thrombus formation. Understanding that rivaroxaban is a member of the broader Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors category helps clinicians select appropriate anticoagulant therapy and appreciate the shared mechanism of action among these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 543
retrieved_docs.content: The heparin group antithrombotic drugs is a class of anticoagulant agents that includes heparin, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin and tinzaparin, as well as antithrombin III. All of these substances are members of the heparin group antithrombotic drugs and share the common mechanism of potentiating antithrombin activity to inhibit clot formation, making them central to the prevention and treatment of thromboembolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors are a class of antithrombotic agents that act by inhibiting factor Xa, and this class includes drugs such as rivaroxaban. Heparin group antithrombotic drugs also belong to the antithrombotic agents category; they exert their effect through antithrombin III and comprise specific members such as dalteparin, nadroparin, and tinzaparin. Other antithrombotic agents in the ATC classification are likewise antithrombotic agents and encompass compounds such as fondaparinux and dermatansulfát. Together, these classes and individual agents represent the major pharmacologic options for inhibiting coagulation through different mechanisms—direct factor Xa inhibition, potentiation of antithrombin III, and synthetic pentasaccharide activity—providing clinicians with a range of therapeutic choices for preventing and treating thrombotic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 605
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors are classified as antithrombotic agents and function through the inhibition of coagulation factor Xa, a key step in the clotting cascade. This class of drugs exerts its therapeutic effect by directly targeting factor Xa activity, thereby reducing thrombus formation. Rivaroxaban is a specific medication that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors class, exemplifying the use of this mechanism in clinical practice for the prevention and treatment of thromboembolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 378
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin group antithrombotic drugs constitute a class of anticoagulant medications that achieve their therapeutic effect primarily through interaction with antithrombin III, a plasma protein that inhibits clotting factors. Within this drug class, dalteparin and nadroparin are specific low‑molecular‑weight heparin agents that share the same mechanism of enhancing antithrombin III activity to prevent thrombus formation. Recognizing dalteparin and nadroparin as members of the heparin group clarifies their pharmacologic relationship and guides clinicians in selecting appropriate antithrombotic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors, Direct thrombin inhibitors, Heparin group antithrombotic drugs, and Other antithrombotic agents in ATC are all classified as antithrombotic agents. Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors exert their therapeutic effect through inhibition of factor Xa, a relationship described as “inhibitory factor Xa.” Rivaroxaban is a specific drug that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors class. Direct thrombin inhibitors include argatroban as a representative member. The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs comprise several low‑molecular‑weight heparins and related compounds, such as enoxaparin, dalteparin, and tinzaparin, as well as the endogenous protein antithrombin III, all of which are members of this heparin‑based class. Other antithrombotic agents in ATC encompass drugs like fondaparinux and dermatansulfát, which are categorized separately from the heparin group but share the overall antithrombotic function. Together, these classes and their individual members illustrate the diverse mechanisms and agents used to prevent and treat thrombotic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2008
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin group antithrombotic drugs encompass a family of anticoagulant agents that share a common mechanism of enhancing antithrombin activity. Members of this group include the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin and tinzaparin, as well as the plasma protein antithrombin III. All of these substances are classified within the heparin group and are used to prevent and treat thrombotic disorders by potentiating the inhibition of clotting factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 130
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors are a subclass of antithrombotic agents that act through an inhibitory faktoru Xa mechanism, thereby preventing the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin. Rivaroxaban is a specific drug that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors class. The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs also fall under the broader category of antithrombotic agents; they exert their anticoagulant effect primarily by enhancing the activity of antithrombin III and include low‑molecular‑weight heparins such as enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin, and tinzaparin. Other antithrombotic agents listed in the ATC classification comprise fondaparinux, a synthetic pentasaccharide that selectively inhibits factor Xa, and dermatansulfát, a sulfated glycosaminoglycan with anticoagulant properties. Together, Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors, Heparin group antithrombotic drugs, and these other ATC antithrombotic agents represent distinct but related therapeutic options for preventing thrombus formation, each characterized by unique molecular targets and representative drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin group antithrombotic drugs are a class of anticoagulant agents that function primarily by potentiating the activity of antithrombin III, a natural plasma protein that inhibits several clotting factors. Within this group, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin and tinzaparin are recognized as individual members and are widely used for prophylaxis and treatment of thromboembolic disorders. Although antithrombin III is not itself a drug, it is a critical component of the heparin mechanism of action, and its interaction with these heparin agents underlies their therapeutic effect. Understanding that enoxaparin, dalteparin and tinzaparin belong to the heparin antithrombotic class, and that antithrombin III serves as the essential cofactor, is important for appropriate selection and management of anticoagulant therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 643
retrieved_docs.content: The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs is a class that includes heparin, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin and tinzaparin, as well as antithrombin III. All of these agents share the common mechanism of potentiating antithrombin activity to inhibit clot formation and are used to prevent and treat thromboembolic disorders. In clinical practice, heparin may be employed in the management of cerebral infarction (احتشاء دماغي), underscoring the therapeutic relevance of this drug class for acute stroke and other conditions where rapid anticoagulation is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors, Direct thrombin inhibitors, Heparin‑group antithrombotic drugs, and Other antithrombotic agents in ATC are all subclasses of antithrombotic agents. Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors exert their effect by inhibiting factor Xa, a key enzyme in the coagulation cascade, and include rivaroxaban as a representative member of this class. Direct thrombin inhibitors act on thrombin to prevent clot formation and comprise drugs such as argatroban and dabigatran etexilát. Heparin‑group antithrombotic drugs enhance the activity of antithrombin III and encompass agents like enoxaparin, dalteparin, tinzaparin, and antithrombin III itself. Other antithrombotic agents in ATC contain drugs such as fondaparinux and dermatansulfát, which are classified separately from the heparin group. Together, these categories provide a spectrum of mechanisms for preventing and treating thrombosis, and recognizing their relationships helps clinicians choose appropriate therapy based on the specific pharmacologic target and drug characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: endovascular intracranial administration, where non‑thrombolytic pharmacologic agents are explicitly excluded, underscoring the need for agent‑specific selection. Within the broader antithrombotic drug family, direct Factor Xa inhibitors (e.g., rivaroxaban), direct thrombin inhibitors (argatroban, dabigatran etexilate) and the heparin group—including unfractionated heparin, low‑molecular‑weight heparins (enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin, tinzaparin) and the cofactor antithrombin III—share the therapeutic goal of preventing clot formation. Ophthalmic agents placed in the ATC subclass “Other ophthalmologicals” include Merkaptamin, inosin, ethylmorfin, potassium iodide (mechanism unknown) and riboflavin, illustrating cross‑disciplinary classification. Safety considerations emphasize accidental poisoning by fibrinolytics and drug‑induced levin dissolution poisoning, highlighting the importance of careful handling and dosing. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 747
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin group antithrombotic drugs and Other antithrombotic agents in ATC are both classified as antithrombotic agents. The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs category encompasses several low‑molecular‑weight heparins and related compounds, including enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin, tinzaparin, and the protein antithrombin III, all of which function by enhancing inhibition of clotting factors. In contrast, the Other antithrombotic agents in ATC category includes agents such as fondaparinux, a synthetic pentasaccharide that selectively inhibits factor Xa, and dermatansulfát, another distinct antithrombotic compound. Together, these drug groups represent the major subclasses of antithrombotic therapy used to prevent and treat thromboembolic disorders.
